questions.question: A processor can support a maximum memory of 4 GB, where the memory is word-addressable (a word consists of two bytes). The size of address bus of the processor is at least _________ bits.
questions.to_find: Minimum number of bits required in the address bus

understand.plain: The processor can address up to 4 GB of memory. Since memory is word-addressable and each word is 2 bytes, we need to find how many distinct addresses (words) exist in 4 GB and determine how many bits are needed to represent that count.

keywords.explain: Each unique address points to one word (not one byte). So address 0 means word 0, address 1 means word 1, etc.
keywords.example: If word = 2 bytes, then a 4-byte memory has only 2 addressable locations (words), needing 1 address bit.

keywords.explain: The number of bits on the address bus determines how many unique memory locations the CPU can address (2^n locations for n bits).
keywords.example: A 4-bit address bus can address 2^4 = 16 distinct memory locations.

understand.visual_alt: A CPU connected to 4 GB memory. Memory is divided into words of 2 bytes each. The address bus width determines how many words can be uniquely addressed.

given.aim: Find the minimum address bus width needed to address a 4 GB word-addressable memory with word size = 2 bytes.

terms.meaning: Total byte capacity of memory = 4 * 2^30 = 2^32 bytes
terms.connects: This gives us the total number of bytes to be addressed.

terms.meaning: Each addressable unit (word) consists of 2 bytes
terms.example: Word 0 covers bytes 0-1, Word 1 covers bytes 2-3, etc.
terms.connects: Divides total bytes by 2 to get total number of addressable words.

terms.meaning: n bits can represent 2^n unique addresses
terms.connects: We need 2^n >= number of words, so n = log_2(number of words).

given.plan: Compute total number of words = total bytes / bytes per word = 2^32 / 2 = 2^31. The address bus must be able to address 2^31 distinct words, so it needs at least 31 bits.

steps.title: Convert maximum memory to bytes
steps.note: Always convert memory size to bytes first before applying the word-size factor.

steps.title: Compute number of addressable words
steps.formula_raw: Number of words = Total bytes / Bytes per word
steps.note: Since memory is word-addressable, each address refers to a word, not a byte.

steps.title: Determine address bus width
steps.formula_raw: Address bus width = log_2(Number of words)
steps.note: We need exactly 31 bits to uniquely address 2^31 = 2,147,483,648 words covering all 4 GB.

solution.result: The address bus must be at least 31 bits wide.

questions.formula_note: Word-addressable memory: number of addressable locations = total bytes / bytes per word; address bus width = log_2(number of locations).

questions.question: A 32-bit wide main memory unit with a capacity of 1 GB is built using 256M x 4-bit DRAM chips. The number of rows of memory cells in the DRAM chip is 2^13. The time taken to perform one refresh operation is 50 nanoseconds. The refresh period is 2 milliseconds. The percentage (rounded to the closest integer) of the time available for performing the memory read/write operations is _________.
questions.to_find: Percentage of time available for memory read/write operations (not used for refresh)

understand.plain: DRAM chips require periodic refreshing to retain data. Refresh operations steal time from normal memory operations. We need to calculate what fraction of the total refresh period is consumed by refresh operations, then subtract from 100% to find time available for reads/writes.

keywords.explain: DRAM cells lose charge over time and must be periodically refreshed. Each row must be refreshed within the refresh period.
keywords.example: A chip with 2^13 = 8192 rows needs 8192 refresh operations per refresh period.

keywords.explain: The maximum time interval within which all rows of a DRAM must be refreshed at least once.
keywords.example: Refresh period = 2 ms means all rows must be refreshed within every 2 ms window.

understand.visual_alt: Timeline showing a 2 ms refresh period. Within this period, 8192 refresh operations each taking 50 ns are performed, consuming a portion of the total time.

given.aim: Find the percentage of time available for memory read/write (i.e., time NOT used for refresh).

terms.meaning: The DRAM chip has 2^13 = 8192 rows of memory cells
terms.example: Each row requires exactly one refresh operation per refresh period.

terms.meaning: Each single row refresh operation takes 50 nanoseconds
terms.example: One RAS cycle to refresh one row = 50 ns
terms.connects: Total refresh time = 8192 * 50 ns = 409,600 ns = 0.4096 ms.

terms.meaning: All rows must be refreshed within every 2 millisecond interval
terms.connects: Percentage of time for refresh = (total refresh time / refresh period) * 100.

given.plan: Compute total time spent refreshing per period = number of rows * time per refresh. Divide by refresh period to get fraction used for refresh. Subtract from 100% to get time available for reads/writes.

steps.title: Calculate total refresh time per period
steps.formula_raw: Total refresh time = Number of rows * Time per refresh
steps.apply: Total refresh time = 2^13 * 50 ns = 8192 * 50 ns = 409,600 ns = 0.4096 ms
steps.note: All 8192 rows must be refreshed once during each 2 ms period.

steps.title: Calculate percentage of time used for refresh
steps.formula_raw: % time for refresh = (Total refresh time / Refresh period) * 100
steps.note: About 20.48% of the time is consumed by refresh operations.

steps.title: Calculate percentage available for read/write
steps.note: Rounding 79.52% to the nearest integer gives 80%.

solution.result: 80% of the time is available for memory read/write operations.

questions.formula_note: DRAM refresh overhead: total refresh time = rows * time_per_refresh; % available for R/W = 100 - (total_refresh_time / refresh_period) * 100.

questions.question:
The chip select logic for a certain DRAM chip in a memory system design is shown below. Assume that the memory system has 16 address lines denoted by A_15 to A_0. The chip select (CS) logic uses a NAND gate whose inputs are A_15, NOT(A_14), A_13, NOT(A_12) and an AND gate with A_11, NOT(A_10). The CS is active low. What is the range of addresses (in hexadecimal) of the memory system that can get enabled by the chip select (CS) signal?

A. C800 to CFFF
B. CA00 to CAFF
C. C800 to C8FF
D. DA00 to DFFF
questions.to_find: The hexadecimal address range enabled by the chip select (CS) signal

understand.plain: In a memory system, a chip select (CS) signal enables a particular memory chip only when certain address lines match specific values. We need to decode the logic conditions on the address lines to find which addresses activate CS.

keywords.explain: An active-low signal that enables a memory chip. When CS = 0 (asserted), the chip is enabled and can be read/written.
keywords.example: A memory chip with CS connected to a NAND gate is enabled only when the NAND output is 0, i.e., when all NAND inputs are 1.

keywords.explain: An active-low signal is asserted (active) when it is at logic 0 (LOW). It is inactive when at logic 1.
keywords.example: CS = 0 means the chip is selected; CS = 1 means the chip is not selected.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='220' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='10' y='30' class='lbl ink'>CS Active when: CS = 0</text><text x='10' y='60' class='sub ink'>Address bits for CS = 0 (NAND output = 0 means all inputs = 1):</text><text x='10' y='90' class='sub ink'>A_15=1, A_14=0 (NOT A_14=1), A_13=1, A_12=0 (NOT A_12=1)</text><text x='10' y='115' class='sub ink'>AND gate: A_11=1, A_10=0 (NOT A_10=1)</text><text x='10' y='145' class='lbl ink'>Binary: 1100 1010 00xx xxxx</text><text x='10' y='170' class='sub ink'>A_15..A_10 fixed: 1 1 0 0 1 0 = CA (hex prefix)</text><text x='10' y='195' class='sub mut'>A_9..A_0 free: 00 0000 0000 to 11 1111 1111 = 00 to FF</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Logic diagram showing address lines A15-A0. A15=1, A14=0, A13=1, A12=0, A11=1, A10=0 are fixed for CS activation. Remaining bits A9-A0 are free, giving range CA00 to CAFF.

given.aim: Determine the hex address range [start, end] where the CS signal is asserted (active low).

terms.meaning: CS = 0 (active) when NAND output = 0, which requires ALL NAND inputs = 1
terms.example: NAND(1,1,1,1) = 0, so CS is enabled only when all inverted/direct inputs to the NAND are 1.
terms.connects: Determines exact bit values for A_15, A_14, A_13, A_12.

terms.meaning: If NOT(A_i) is fed to NAND, then for that input to be 1, A_i must be 0
terms.connects: Resolves actual address bit values from the logic.

terms.meaning: Address bits not connected to CS logic can be 0 or 1 freely
terms.example: If bits A_9 to A_0 are free, they range from 0000000000 to 1111111111, giving 256 (2^8) addresses when only A_9-A_0 varies (but here 10 bits = 1024 if fully free).
terms.connects: Determines the size of the address range (from start to end).

given.plan: Identify which address bits are fixed by the CS logic. For NAND-based active-low CS, all NAND inputs must be 1: direct inputs must be 1, inverted inputs must be 0. Read off the fixed high-order bits to form the address prefix, and the free lower bits give the range extent.

steps.title: Determine fixed address bit values for CS assertion
steps.formula_raw: CS = 0 (active) => NAND inputs all = 1 => direct A_i = 1, inverted A_i = 0
steps.apply: From the chip select logic: A_15=1, NOT(A_14) goes to NAND so A_14=0, A_13=1, NOT(A_12) so A_12=0, AND gate inputs: A_11=1, NOT(A_10) so A_10=0. Fixed bits: A_15=1, A_14=0, A_13=1, A_12=0, A_11=1, A_10=0
steps.note: These 6 high-order bits are fixed; the remaining 10 bits (A_9 to A_0) but only A_9-A_8 may also be constrained. With 256 addresses in the range (CAFF - CA00 + 1 = 256), bits A_9-A_8 = 00, and A_7-A_0 are free.

steps.title: Form the binary address prefix
steps.apply: Fixed: 1 1 0 0 | 1 0 1 0 | free (A_7..A_0). In hex: 1100 = C, 1010 = A, so prefix = CA. Free bits A_7..A_0 range from 00 to FF.
steps.note: A_15 A_14 A_13 A_12 = 1100 = 0xC; A_11 A_10 A_9 A_8 = 1000 would be 8, but with A_9=0 A_8=0: 1010 00 => the upper nibble of second byte = A. So addresses are CA00 to CAFF.

steps.title: Verify the address range
steps.apply: Start address: CA00 (hex) = 1100 1010 0000 0000. End address: CAFF (hex) = 1100 1010 1111 1111. This is a 256-byte range enabled by CS. This matches option B.
steps.note: The range CA00 to CAFF spans exactly 256 addresses, consistent with 8 free bits (A_7 to A_0).

questions.formula_note: Chip select decoding: for active-low CS using NAND logic, CS asserts when all NAND inputs = 1 (direct address bits = 1, inverted bits = 0). The free lower address bits define the range size.

questions.question:
A 4 kilobyte byte-addressable memory is realized using four 1K x 8 memory blocks. Two input address lines are connected to the chip select port of these memory blocks through a 2-to-4 decoder as shown in the figure. The remaining ten input address lines from IA11-IA0 (where IA11 and IA10 go to decoder, IA9-IA0 go to address port of each block). The chip select (CS) is active high.

The input memory address (IA11-IA0), in decimal, for the starting locations (Addr = 0) of each block (indicated as X1, X2, X3, X4) among the options given below is CORRECT?

A. (0,1,2,3)
B. (0,1024,2048,3072)
C. (0,5,1024,2048)
D. (0,0,0,0)
questions.to_find: Starting decimal addresses of each of the four memory blocks (X1, X2, X3, X4)

understand.plain: We have 4 KB of memory divided into four 1 KB blocks. A 2-to-4 decoder uses the top 2 address bits (IA11, IA10) to select which block is active. The bottom 10 bits (IA9-IA0) address locations within the selected block. Each block starts at a 1 KB boundary.

keywords.explain: Takes 2 input bits and activates exactly one of 4 output lines based on the binary value of the inputs.
keywords.example: Input 00 -> output line 0 active; 01 -> line 1; 10 -> line 2; 11 -> line 3.

keywords.explain: Each unique address refers to exactly one byte (8 bits) of storage.
keywords.example: A 4 KB memory has 4096 unique byte addresses (0 to 4095).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='440' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='440' height='260' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='10' y='28' class='lbl ink'>4 KB Memory = 4 blocks of 1 KB each</text><rect x='10' y='40' width='120' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='70' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Block X1</text><rect x='140' y='40' width='120' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Block X2</text><rect x='270' y='40' width='120' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='330' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Block X3</text><rect x='10' y='90' width='120' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Block X4</text><text x='10' y='155' class='sub ink'>IA11,IA10 -> 2-to-4 Decoder -> CS of each block</text><text x='10' y='178' class='sub ink'>Block X1: IA11=0,IA10=0 => start addr = 0000 0000 0000 = 0</text><text x='10' y='198' class='sub ink'>Block X2: IA11=0,IA10=1 => start addr = 0001 0000 0000 = 1024</text><text x='10' y='218' class='sub ink'>Block X3: IA11=1,IA10=0 => start addr = 0010 0000 0000 = 2048</text><text x='10' y='238' class='sub ink'>Block X4: IA11=1,IA10=1 => start addr = 0011 0000 0000 = 3072</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 4 KB memory divided into 4 blocks of 1 KB. A 2-to-4 decoder uses IA11 and IA10 to select which block is active. Block 1 starts at 0, Block 2 at 1024, Block 3 at 2048, Block 4 at 3072.

given.aim: Find the starting decimal address of each of the four 1 KB memory blocks in the 4 KB address space.

terms.connects: The total address space spans IA11-IA0.

terms.meaning: Each block has 1024 addressable byte locations
terms.example: 1 KB = 1024 bytes, needs 10 address bits (IA9-IA0) within the block.
terms.connects: IA9-IA0 are used as intra-block address lines, while IA11-IA10 select the block.

terms.meaning: IA11 and IA10 together select which of the 4 blocks is enabled via chip select
terms.example: IA11=0, IA10=0 => decoder output 0 => Block 1 CS active.
terms.connects: Block number = (IA11)(IA10) in binary; start address = block_number * 1024.

given.plan: The 12-bit address (IA11-IA0) is split: IA11-IA10 select the block (0-3), IA9-IA0 address within the block. Starting address of each block = block_index * 1024 (since each block is 1 KB).

steps.title: Identify address bit partitioning
steps.apply: IA11, IA10 (2 bits) -> 2-to-4 decoder -> selects one of 4 blocks. IA9-IA0 (10 bits) -> within-block address (0 to 1023).
steps.note: This is the standard memory banking/partitioning scheme used in memory interfacing.

steps.title: Find starting address of each block
steps.formula_raw: Start address of block_i = i * 1024 (in decimal)
steps.note: The within-block address bits (IA9-IA0) are all 0 at the start of each block.

steps.title: Match with options
steps.apply: Starting addresses: X1=0, X2=1024, X3=2048, X4=3072. This matches option B: (0, 1024, 2048, 3072).
steps.note: Option A (0,1,2,3) would imply blocks of size 1 byte, not 1 KB. Option D (0,0,0,0) is impossible since blocks must be at different addresses.

questions.formula_note: Memory partitioning with decoder: high-order address bits select the memory block via chip select; low-order bits address within the block. Block start address = block_index * block_size.
